content.text: Terminal execution is the capability that lets a coding agent run arbitrary shell commands and receive their output as structured text. It is typically exposed as a single tool — commonly called bash or run_command — that accepts a command string, spawns a child process, waits for it to finish (or times out), and returns the combined stdout and stderr together with the exit code.

This deceptively simple interface unlocks an enormous range of capabilities: running tests, executing build scripts, calling CLIs, installing packages, inspecting process state, reading file metadata, and invoking any other tool the operating system provides. For a coding agent, terminal execution is the escape hatch from pure text manipulation into the full power of the software development environment.

Many software engineering tasks are verifiable only by running something. An agent that can only read and edit files must rely on static analysis or the model's training knowledge to judge whether a change is correct. An agent that can also execute commands can run the test suite, observe the actual output, and iterate based on real feedback. This closes the edit-verify loop that defines competent software engineering.

Terminal execution also allows the agent to use existing tools rather than re-implementing their logic in prompts. Instead of asking the model to simulate what mypy would say about a type annotation, the agent runs mypy src/ and reads the actual errors. This dramatically improves accuracy and reduces the risk of hallucinated tool outputs — a failure mode that is invisible to the model but catastrophic in practice.
Running tests, linters, and type checkers gives the agent actual error messages rather than modelled predictions, enabling precise targeted fixes.
The entire ecosystem of CLI tools — git, npm, make, docker, curl — is available without any custom implementation inside the agent.
After editing code, the agent can immediately verify correctness by running the relevant test, catching regressions before they reach the user.
Commands like python --version, pip list, or env give the agent ground truth about the runtime environment, preventing version-mismatch errors.

Complex multi-step workflows — dependency installation, database migrations, code generation — can be expressed as shell commands that the agent executes in sequence, adapting each step based on previous output.

Unrestricted shell access is one of the highest-risk capabilities an agent can possess. A single malformed command can delete files, exfiltrate data, install malware, or exhaust system resources. The tool implementation must therefore enforce safety constraints at multiple layers: input validation, command blocklists, process isolation, network restrictions, and filesystem sandboxing. Adding these safety layers also adds latency and complexity.

Terminal execution is also inherently stateful in ways that can surprise agents. The current working directory persists between commands only if they share a shell session; each subprocess.run call starts a fresh shell by default, so cd /tmp in one call does not affect the next. Environment variables set in one call are invisible in subsequent calls unless the tool maintains a persistent shell session or re-exports them explicitly.

When the agent LLM emits a tool-use block naming the bash tool, the tool implementation receives the command string and executes it using subprocess.Popen. The process runs with stdout and stderr redirected to pipes. A timeout watchdog runs concurrently; if the process exceeds the configured limit, the watchdog sends SIGTERM followed by SIGKILL, then returns a structured timeout error to the agent.

The tool reads stdout and stderr from the pipes as the process runs. Most implementations merge them into a single stream (equivalent to 2>&1) so the agent sees output in the order it was produced. The combined output is capped at a configurable maximum (e.g. 100 KB) to prevent a verbose command from filling the context window; any truncation is signalled with a marker.

When the process exits, the tool packages the output, exit code, and elapsed time into a structured result object and returns it to the agent. The agent then decides whether the command succeeded, what the output means, and what to do next. A non-zero exit code is surfaced as data, not as a Python exception — it is the agent's responsibility to interpret it.

For interactive commands that read from stdin — password prompts, pagers like less, ssh sessions — non-interactive mode must be forced. Tools typically set stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL and inject CI=true and TERM=dumb into the subprocess environment so commands do not wait for user input.

attrs.alt: Architecture showing the coding agent invoking the bash tool which applies sandbox restrictions and spawns a process, collecting output into a capped buffer, with the result returned to the agent context.

content.text: The bash tool acts as a broker between the agent's language model and the operating system. It applies safety restrictions before spawning the process, collects output into a bounded buffer, enforces the timeout, and constructs a structured result. The agent never communicates directly with the OS — all interaction goes through this controlled interface.

Use terminal execution to verify rather than predict. After editing code, run the test suite. After writing a migration, run it against a test database. After modifying a configuration file, run the linter. Anywhere the agent would otherwise need to simulate what a tool would output, it should instead run that tool and read the real output.

Avoid terminal execution for purely read-only information gathering where a dedicated tool exists. Reading a file is better done via a file-read tool than running cat — the dedicated tool is safer, returns structured data, and avoids spawning a subprocess. Reserve terminal execution for tasks that genuinely require process execution.
Define the tool schema with a command string parameter and an optional timeout_seconds integer.
Validate the command before execution: reject commands matching known dangerous patterns.
Spawn the process with subprocess.Popen, piping stdout and stderr, setting stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL, and injecting non-interactive environment variables.
Use process.communicate(timeout=...) to wait for completion. On TimeoutExpired, kill the process group and return a timeout error.
Cap output at MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES and append a truncation marker if exceeded.
Return a structured object containing stdout, exit code, timed-out flag, and elapsed seconds.

# Production-grade bash tool implementation
import subprocess
import os
import signal
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES = 100_000  # ~100 KB cap on combined output
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 30        # seconds

BLOCKED_PATTERNS = [
    "rm -rf /",
    "mkfs",
    "> /dev/sda",
    ":(){ :|:& };:",  # fork bomb
]


@dataclass
class CommandResult:
    command: str
    stdout: str
    exit_code: int
    timed_out: bool
    elapsed_seconds: float

    def to_tool_result(self) -> str:
        status = "TIMEOUT" if self.timed_out else f"exit {self.exit_code}"
        output = self.stdout
        if len(output.encode()) >= MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES:
            output += "\n[...output truncated at 100 KB...]"
        return (
            f"<command>{self.command}</command>\n"
            f"<status>{status}</status>\n"
            f"<elapsed>{self.elapsed_seconds:.2f}s</elapsed>\n"
            f"<output>\n{output}\n</output>"
        )


def run_bash(
    command: str,
    working_dir: str = ".",
    timeout_seconds: int = DEFAULT_TIMEOUT,
    extra_env: dict[str, str] | None = None,
) -> CommandResult:
    """Execute a shell command safely and return structured output."""
    for pattern in BLOCKED_PATTERNS:
        if pattern in command:
            return CommandResult(
                command=command,
                stdout=f"Blocked: command matches '{pattern}'",
                exit_code=1, timed_out=False, elapsed_seconds=0.0,
            )

    env = os.environ.copy()
    env.update({
        "CI": "true",
        "TERM": "dumb",
        "PYTHONUNBUFFERED": "1",
        "DEBIAN_FRONTEND": "noninteractive",
        **(extra_env or {}),
    })

    start = time.monotonic()
    timed_out = False

    try:
        proc = subprocess.Popen(
            command,
            shell=True,
            executable="/bin/bash",
            stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
            stderr=subprocess.STDOUT,  # merge stderr into stdout
            stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL,
            cwd=working_dir,
            env=env,
            start_new_session=True,  # creates a new process group
        )
        try:
            raw_output, _ = proc.communicate(timeout=timeout_seconds)
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            # Kill the entire process group to clean up children
            try:
                os.killpg(os.getpgid(proc.pid), signal.SIGKILL)
            except ProcessLookupError:
                pass
            raw_output, _ = proc.communicate()
            timed_out = True

        exit_code = proc.returncode if not timed_out else -1

    except Exception as exc:
        raw_output = str(exc).encode()
        exit_code = -2

    elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
    raw_output = raw_output[:MAX_OUTPUT_BYTES]
    stdout = raw_output.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")

    return CommandResult(
        command=command, stdout=stdout,
        exit_code=exit_code, timed_out=timed_out, elapsed_seconds=elapsed,
    )


BASH_TOOL: dict[str, Any] = {
    "name": "bash",
    "description": (
        "Execute a shell command in the project directory. "
        "Returns stdout+stderr and exit code. Use for running tests, linters, "
        "build commands, and git operations."
    ),
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "command": {"type": "string", "description": "Shell command to execute"},
            "timeout_seconds": {"type": "integer", "default": 30},
        },
        "required": ["command"],
    },
}
The following example shows how a coding agent uses terminal execution to run tests after making a code change, reads the pytest output, and iterates on any failures.
A second example shows a persistent shell session using pexpect, where cd and export persist across multiple commands within a single agent task.

# Persistent shell session using pexpect for stateful execution
# pip install pexpect
import pexpect

PROMPT_SENTINEL = "__AGENT_DONE__"

class PersistentShell:
    """Bash session where cd and export persist across calls."""

    def __init__(self, cwd: str = "."):
        self.shell = pexpect.spawn(
            "/bin/bash", ["--norc", "--noprofile"],
            cwd=cwd, encoding="utf-8", timeout=60,
        )
        self.shell.sendline(f'PS1="{PROMPT_SENTINEL} "')
        self.shell.expect(PROMPT_SENTINEL)

    def run(self, command: str, timeout: int = 30) -> tuple[str, int]:
        self.shell.sendline(command)
        self.shell.expect(PROMPT_SENTINEL, timeout=timeout)
        output = self.shell.before.strip()
        self.shell.sendline("echo $?")
        self.shell.expect(PROMPT_SENTINEL)
        try:
            exit_code = int(self.shell.before.strip().splitlines()[-1])
        except (ValueError, IndexError):
            exit_code = -1
        return output, exit_code

    def close(self):
        self.shell.sendline("exit")
        self.shell.close()


# Usage: cd persists to the next command
shell = PersistentShell(cwd="/home/user/myproject")
shell.run("cd src")                         # working dir changes
out, code = shell.run("python -m pytest -x -q")
print(f"exit={code}\n{out}")
shell.close()

A CI-integrated coding agent receives a failing build notification. It uses terminal execution to run the failing test in isolation, reads the traceback, edits the offending function, reruns the test to confirm the fix, then runs the full test suite and the linter before reporting success. Each command's output informs the next decision.

# Edit-verify cycle: run failing test, fix, rerun, check regressions
def fix_failing_test(test_id: str, project_root: str) -> str:
    shell = PersistentShell(cwd=project_root)

    # Step 1: get the failure traceback
    output, code = shell.run(f"python -m pytest {test_id} -x --tb=short 2>&1")
    if code == 0:
        shell.close()
        return "Test is already passing."

    # (agent reads relevant source files and generates a fix here)

    # Step 2: verify the specific test now passes
    output, code = shell.run(f"python -m pytest {test_id} -x --tb=short 2>&1")
    if code != 0:
        shell.close()
        return f"Fix attempt failed:\n{output}"

    # Step 3: regression check on full suite
    output, code = shell.run("python -m pytest --tb=short -q 2>&1")
    if code != 0:
        shell.close()
        return f"Fix introduced regressions:\n{output}"

    # Step 4: linter
    out_lint, code_lint = shell.run("ruff check src/ 2>&1")
    lint_status = "clean" if code_lint == 0 else f"issues:\n{out_lint}"

    shell.close()
    return f"Fix verified. Full suite green. Linter: {lint_status}"

The most dangerous mistake is running the bash tool without any sandboxing and trusting the LLM to avoid destructive commands. LLMs can and do generate commands that delete files, overwrite configs, or make outbound network requests. Safety constraints must be enforced in the tool implementation — model behaviour is not a reliable safety mechanism.

A common correctness mistake is assuming that cd in one call persists to the next when using stateless subprocess invocations. Each subprocess.run(shell=True) call starts a fresh shell. Combine directory change and command in a single call: cd src && python test.py. Failing to kill the process group on timeout leaves orphan subprocesses consuming resources — always use os.killpg when start_new_session=True is set.
Even innocuous commands can hang. A 30-second default with a configurable override handles the vast majority of cases.
Most diagnostic output — compiler errors, test failures — is written to stderr. Merging gives the agent a single ordered stream matching what a human sees in the terminal.
A verbose build can produce megabytes. Cap at 50-100 KB and append a truncation notice; the agent can then re-run with --quiet to narrow the output.
Set CI=true, TERM=dumb, and DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive to prevent tools from blocking on prompts.
Return the exit code as data.
Include the exit code in the tool result so the agent can distinguish silent success (exit 0) from silent failure (exit 1).
Always set the subprocess cwd to the project root so relative paths in commands resolve predictably.

Each bash tool invocation carries subprocess spawn overhead of 5-20ms on Linux. For an agent that runs dozens of commands per task, this is negligible — the dominant latency is the command itself (test suites, builds, package installations).

For long-running processes, streaming output to the agent as it is produced provides useful partial feedback and avoids the need to wait for full completion. Most current coding agents process output only after command completion, which is simpler and sufficient for typical tasks.
Independent commands — running the linter and the type checker simultaneously — can run in parallel, cutting wall-clock time roughly in half.
Container-based sandboxes add 50-500ms of startup overhead per command. Re-using a warm container across commands in a session is essential for interactive agents.
Zero dependencies; new shell per call; no state persistence
Maintains cd/env state; suited for multi-step workflows
Strong isolation; reusable warm containers; network control
Non-blocking; allows streaming output; pairs with async agent loops

Terminal execution is the highest-risk tool a coding agent can possess. The attack surface has two vectors: a malicious user crafting a prompt that causes the agent to execute a destructive command (prompt injection), and a buggy agent that autonomously generates a harmful command. Defence must be layered: input validation at the tool boundary, process-level sandboxing (container or namespace isolation), capability restrictions (no network, read-only mounts except the workspace), and audit logging of every command executed.

Reliability concerns centre on process hygiene. Always set a timeout and kill the process tree on expiry — not just the parent process — otherwise orphan subprocesses accumulate, consuming memory and CPU. Use os.killpg(os.getpgid(proc.pid), signal.SIGKILL) rather than proc.kill() to terminate the entire process group. Clean up temporary files written by commands in a finally block to avoid disk exhaustion across long agent sessions.
Terminal execution closes the edit-verify loop: the agent runs the test suite, reads real failure messages, and iterates rather than guessing at correctness.

The bash tool interface is simple — command string in, text output and exit code out — but the implementation requires careful attention to timeouts, output capping, non-interactive mode, and sandboxing.
Stateless subprocess calls do not preserve working directory state between calls; combine cd && command in a single call or use a persistent shell session when state persistence is needed.
Merge stdout and stderr, cap output at ~100 KB, and always include the exit code in the tool result.
Safety constraints must be implemented in the tool itself — sandbox the process, restrict network access, and log every command. Model behaviour is not a reliable safety mechanism.
Kill the entire process group on timeout to prevent orphan processes from accumulating.
Reserve terminal execution for tasks requiring process execution; use dedicated tools for file reads and writes to minimise the attack surface.
